__comment: EuroTransit order-lifecycle dashboard — follows accepted orders through the observable async stages: Payments authorization, Kafka consumers, Notifications completion, resilience controls, and Tempo traces. This is a convergence/operations view, not a replacement for database reconciliation: the applications do not yet expose bounded-cardinality order-state counts or age-of-oldest-in-flight metrics.
title: EuroTransit — Order lifecycle

options.content:
### Accepted is not completed
`POST /orders` returns **202** before the Kafka workflow finishes. Compare accepted orders with authorization and notification progress, then use per-topic lag and the Tempo table below to locate a stalled stage.

**Important:** the completion percentage is an operational convergence proxy, not an SLO. Range boundaries, legitimate `FAILED` orders, and backlog drain can make it lower than or temporarily higher than 100%. Exact `DRAFT` / `RESERVED` / `CONFIRMED` / `FAILED` counts and oldest-in-flight age require additional bounded-cardinality application metrics; never add `order_id` to Prometheus labels.

panels.title: New orders accepted
panels.description: New workflow instances accepted in the selected range. Counts HTTP 202 only; idempotent replays return 200 and are deliberately excluded.

targets.expr: sum(increase(http_server_requests_seconds_count{namespace="eurotransit", job=~"eurotransit-orders(-canary)?", uri="/orders", method="POST", status="202"}[$__range])) or vector(0)

panels.title: Authorization responses
panels.description: Successful Payments authorization responses in the selected range. This counts calls, not unique orders: bounded retries and idempotent replays can make it exceed accepted orders.

targets.expr: sum(increase(http_server_requests_seconds_count{namespace="eurotransit", job="eurotransit-payments", uri="/payments/authorize", method="POST", status=~"2.."}[$__range])) or vector(0)

panels.title: Notifications sent
panels.description: Durably deduplicated notification completions in the selected range. Notifications are outside the checkout success criterion but are the final observable stage.

panels.title: Notification / accepted proxy
panels.description: Notifications sent divided by newly accepted orders over the selected range. Operational convergence proxy only: not an SLI or business success rate, and range-edge/backlog effects can exceed 100%.

targets.expr: 100 * (sum(increase(notifications_sent_total{namespace="eurotransit"}[$__range])) or vector(0)) / clamp_min((sum(increase(http_server_requests_seconds_count{namespace="eurotransit", job=~"eurotransit-orders(-canary)?", uri="/orders", method="POST", status="202"}[$__range])) or vector(0)), 1)

panels.title: Controlled refusals
panels.description: HTTP 429 responses in the selected range. These are deliberate load shedding, excluded from the ratified checkout error-budget numerator.

targets.expr: sum(increase(http_server_requests_seconds_count{namespace="eurotransit", job=~"eurotransit-orders(-canary)?", uri="/orders", method="POST", status="429"}[$__range])) or vector(0)

panels.title: Guard interventions
panels.description: Times the compensation guard declined to release seats because a competing path had already confirmed the order. Non-zero means the guard prevented a correctness race and deserves investigation.

panels.title: Lifecycle throughput — accepted → authorized → notified
panels.description: The observable workflow funnel over time. Persistent divergence after allowing for normal async delay indicates a stalled stage or terminal failures. Authorization is call-based and may include safe retries; Notifications is terminal and deduplicated.

panels.title: Kafka stage lag — consumer and topic
panels.description: Backlog at each async edge. Topic and consumer are preserved so the stalled lifecycle stage is visible. No data is not equivalent to zero: application-side lag gauges disappear when a consumer is fully down, so correlate with the service-availability panel.

panels.title: Pipeline service availability
panels.description: At least one Prometheus scrape target is up for each lifecycle service. This is the companion to client-side Kafka lag: if a consumer disappears entirely, its lag gauge can disappear too.

panels.title: Payments circuit breaker — state per Orders pod
panels.description: CLOSED = normal; OPEN = fast-fail with the order remaining RESERVED for bounded redelivery; HALF_OPEN = recovery probe. Per-pod state matters because every Orders replica owns an independent breaker.

targets.expr: max by (pod) (resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state{namespace="eurotransit", name="payments", state="open"}) + 2 * max by (pod) (resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state{namespace="eurotransit", name="payments", state="half_open"})

panels.title: Payments authorization p95
panels.description: Latency of the only synchronous cross-service call on the lifecycle. A rise here explains slow/redelivered RESERVED orders even when the initial POST /orders response remains fast.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(http_server_requests_seconds_bucket{namespace="eurotransit", job="eurotransit-payments", uri="/payments/authorize", method="POST"}[5m])))

panels.title: Payments authorization outcomes
panels.description: Authorization response rate split by HTTP status. A collapse in 2xx with an OPEN breaker indicates fast-fail containment; sustained 5xx explains orders parked in RESERVED and redelivered.

panels.title: Order API traces in the selected range
panels.description: Tempo traces rooted in Orders whose root operation contains 'orders'. Open a trace to inspect HTTP and Kafka spans across Inventory, Payments, Orders confirmation, and Notifications. The current instrumentation propagates one trace across the workflow but does not attach order.id as a searchable span attribute.
